element_introduction_order.element: Basic volleyball mechanics (serve, receive, spike, block, dive)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Daytona Beach (first American Circuit match) - introduces human opponents with basic skill levels

element_introduction_order.element: Team selection and character stats differentiation
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: American Circuit progression - players learn which team compositions suit their playstyle (balanced, powerful/slow, defensive, or fast)

element_introduction_order.element: Progressive opponent difficulty and tactical complexity
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: World Cup (Hard difficulty) - opponents employ more sophisticated blocking and spiking strategies

element_introduction_order.element: Set and match system (multiple rounds per match)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Demonstrated throughout tournament progression - players must sustain performance across multiple sets

element_introduction_order.element: Special court environments and cosmetic variations
element_introduction_order.first_appears: Daytona Beach through Los Angeles in American Circuit
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Hawaii and naval/Soviet courts in World Cup - introduces thematic variation without mechanical changes

typical_relief_triggers: Completing a match and returning to main menu
Exercise Mode victory (crowdless, lowest pressure environment)
typical_tension_triggers: Opponent approaching match/set victory (tension theme music plays in Famicom version; notably absent in Exercise Mode)
World Cup progression with fixed hard difficulty
Final matches against Navy and USSR teams
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Escalating tension structure: Exercise Mode (training/warmup) → American Circuit (moderate build) → World Cup (sustained high intensity with fixed opponents and hard difficulty). NES version explicitly designed three difficulty courses rather than single adjustable setting, encouraging linear progression through tension zones.

spatial_grammar.secret_convention: Hidden female teams (accessible only via Game Genie cheat codes), indicating developer content not intended for standard playthrough. Hidden Track Ball controller option (access: Configuration screen Music option + A+B on Controller 2 + Select/Down on Controller 1).

difficulty_curve.early_game: Exercise Mode: single match against Daytona team, crowdless court, lowest difficulty, no tension theme during winning sequences. Designed as explicit practice/tutorial environment.
difficulty_curve.mid_game: American Circuit: five matches (Daytona, New York, Chicago, Las Vegas, Los Angeles) with average/normal difficulty. Introduces geographic variation and progressive opponent skill.
difficulty_curve.late_game: World Cup: seven matches (Japan, Italy, Australia, Mexico, Brazil, Navy, USSR) with hard difficulty and fixed opponent nationalities. Sustained high-intensity challenge with thematic escalation (naval base, Soviet Kremlin redesigned to appear less hostile in NES version).
notable_spikes: Transition from American Circuit to World Cup (difficulty jump from normal to hard)
Navy match (special environment, increased tactical complexity)
USSR final match (narrative climax, redesigned threatening backdrop in Famicom version, toned down in NES)

notable_level_patterns.description: Each court location is distinct visually (Daytona clouds/mascot, New York cherry sign, Chicago pizza scoreboard, Las Vegas color cycling, Los Angeles USC/USA logo, Hawaii crowd/ice cream sign, Navy aircraft carrier, USSR Kremlin). Mechanical gameplay remains consistent; variation is environmental/narrative rather than systemic.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: American Circuit matches 1-5 establish geography; World Cup matches add international representation; Exercise Mode is abstract/crowdless reference court.

notable_level_patterns.description: Famicom version: 10 matches total (5 American + 3 random World Cup + 2 fixed Navy/USSR). NES version: restructured into three separate courses (Exercise 1 match, American Circuit 5 matches, World Cup 7 matches). NES version uses fixed World Cup opponents instead of random assignment.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: American Circuit → World Cup represents the core progression model

notable_level_patterns.description: Four selectable pairs with stat distributions: George/Murphy (balanced), Al/John (powerful/slow/poor defense), Billy/Jimmy (defensive/weak spiking, Double Dragon cameo), Ed/Michael (fast/average). No difficulty-scaling of opponent AI; instead, player must match team composition to playstyle or face stat disadvantages.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Same opponent plays differently depending on player's selected team stats

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Super Spike V'Ball detailed regional differences, game modes, tournament structure, character select, courts, and music)
wikipedia (U.S. Championship V'Ball - arcade origin, NES adaptation changes, female teams, character descriptions, gameplay modes)
